claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 21 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 15 years old and does not live with you.
  - Charlie is 11 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 21, which is 20 or over
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 15 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 11 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 21, which is 20 or over; Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit. To do this properly, I'll need to gather some information about your situation and your children.

Let me start with the first question:

**How many children do you have that you'd like me to assess for Child Benefit?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! I'd like to check if I'm eligible for Child Benefit. I have 3 children I'd like to ask about.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'll help you assess eligibility for all three children.

Before I proceed, let me first review the official eligibility criteria to ensure I ask you the right questions.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I have the official guidance. Let me start gathering information about your three children. I'll ask one question at a time.

**What are the names of your three children?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: My three children are Alex, Blake, and Charlie.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you! So I'm assessing eligibility for Alex, Blake, and Charlie.

**My first question: What are the ages of Alex, Blake, and Charlie?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is 21, Blake is 15, and Charlie is 11.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information.

Since Alex is 21, I need to clarify their current situation. According to the rules, Child Benefit normally stops at age 16, but can continue until age 20 in certain circumstances.

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any education or training at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that.

Now let me ask about Blake and Charlie's living arrangements.

**Do Blake and Charlie both live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Charlie lives with me, but Blake doesn't live with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Let me gather more details about Blake's situation.

**Where does Blake live, and are you making any financial contributions towards Blake's upkeep (such as food, clothes, pocket money, or housing)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake lives with their other parent. I do contribute a bit towards their upkeep - I help out with some things like clothes and pocket money.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. I need to understand the level of your contributions more precisely, as this is important for Blake's eligibility.

**Are your contributions to Blake's upkeep (clothes, pocket money, etc.) at least equal to the amount of Child Benefit you would receive for Blake?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: How much is the Child Benefit amount exactly? I want to make sure I'm giving you the right answer.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's a fair question! However, the exact Child Benefit amounts can vary depending on whether it's your first child or subsequent children, and they can change. 

For the purposes of this eligibility assessment, what matters is whether your contributions are **at least equal to** whatever the current Child Benefit payment rate is for Blake.

To give you a sense of scale: Child Benefit rates are typically in the region of £20-£25+ per week per child, but I'd recommend checking the current rates on the GOV.UK website if you need the exact figure.

**Based on what you're contributing (clothes, pocket money, etc.), would you say your total contributions are at least equal to the standard Child Benefit weekly payment rate?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't think what I contribute adds up to that much. I'd say I give about £10 a week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. That's helpful to know.

Now let me ask about Charlie's situation.

**Does Charlie have any special circumstances I should know about - for example, is Charlie currently in hospital, residential care, or any other type of care arrangement?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Charlie doesn't have any special circumstances like that. They just live at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that clarification.

Now I have enough information to assess eligibility for all three children. Let me review the rules carefully:

Based on the official guidance I've reviewed:

- **Alex (age 21)**: Child Benefit normally stops at age 16. It can only continue until age 20 if the child is in approved education or training. Since Alex is 21 and not in education or training, Alex is ineligible.

- **Blake (age 15)**: Blake lives with their other parent and you contribute approximately £10 per week. According to the rules, you can usually get Child Benefit for 8 weeks after a child goes to live with someone else if nobody else claims. After that, you can continue to get payments only if you contribute at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment itself. Since your contributions (£10/week) are below the Child Benefit threshold, you would only be eligible for the first 8 weeks, after which eligibility would cease unless your contributions increased.

- **Charlie (age 11)**: Charlie lives with you, has no special circumstances, and is under 16. Charlie meets all the basic eligibility criteria.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Summary of Your Eligibility Assessment

Based on the information you've provided and the official Child Benefit eligibility rules, here's the outcome:

| Child | Eligible? | Reason |
|-------|-----------|--------|
| **Alex** (21) | ❌ No | Too old - Child Benefit ends at age 20 and only continues if in approved education or training, which Alex is not. |
| **Blake** (15) | ❌ No | Lives with their other parent, and your contributions (£10/week) fall below the threshold needed to maintain eligibility beyond the initial 8-week period. |
| **Charlie** (11) | ✅ Yes | Meets all criteria - under 16, lives with you, and has no special circumstances affecting eligibility. |

**You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Charlie only.**

If you have any questions about this assessment or your circumstances change, I'd recommend contacting HMRC's Child Benefit Office for further guidance.

reasonings: Alex is 21 years old and not in approved education or training. Child Benefit eligibility normally ends at age 16 and can only continue until age 20 for those in approved education or training. Since Alex no longer meets these criteria, Alex is not eligible.

Blake is 15 and lives with their other parent rather than with you. According to the rules, you can usually get Child Benefit for 8 weeks after your child goes to live with someone else if nobody else claims. After 8 weeks, you can only continue to receive Child Benefit if you contribute at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment itself towards Blake's upkeep. Your contributions are approximately £10 per week, which is below the Child Benefit threshold. Therefore, Blake is not eligible beyond the initial 8-week period.

Charlie is 11 years old and lives with you. Charlie is under 16, you are responsible for Charlie, and there are no special circumstances such as hospitalisation or care that would affect eligibility. Charlie meets all the criteria for Child Benefit eligibility.
arguments.overall_reasoning: Of the three children assessed, only Charlie is eligible for Child Benefit. Alex is ineligible due to being over age 20 and not in approved education or training. Blake is ineligible because although aged 15, Blake lives with their other parent and your financial contributions towards Blake's upkeep fall short of the Child Benefit payment threshold required to maintain eligibility beyond the initial 8-week grace period. Charlie meets all eligibility requirements as a dependent child under 16 living with you with no complications."

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 21 years old and not in approved education or training. Child Benefit eligibility normally ends at age 16 and can only continue until age 20 for those in approved education or training. Since Alex no longer meets these criteria, Alex is not eligible.

Blake is 15 and lives with their other parent rather than with you. According to the rules, you can usually get Child Benefit for 8 weeks after your child goes to live with someone else if nobody else claims. After 8 weeks, you can only continue to receive Child Benefit if you contribute at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment itself towards Blake's upkeep. Your contributions are approximately £10 per week, which is below the Child Benefit threshold. Therefore, Blake is not eligible beyond the initial 8-week period.

Charlie is 11 years old and lives with you. Charlie is under 16, you are responsible for Charlie, and there are no special circumstances such as hospitalisation or care that would affect eligibility. Charlie meets all the criteria for Child Benefit eligibility.

response.overall_reasoning: Of the three children assessed, only Charlie is eligible for Child Benefit. Alex is ineligible due to being over age 20 and not in approved education or training. Blake is ineligible because although aged 15, Blake lives with their other parent and your financial contributions towards Blake's upkeep fall short of the Child Benefit payment threshold required to maintain eligibility beyond the initial 8-week grace period. Charlie meets all eligibility requirements as a dependent child under 16 living with you with no complications."
